volcano_proximity.value: No active volcanoes are near Grand Junction itself. The nearest volcanic feature is the Dotsero maar/volcano (Colorado's only Holocene-age volcano, last erupted ~4,000 years ago), located roughly 100 driving miles east near Eagle County along I-70. Volcanic hazard to Grand Junction is negligible.

hurricane_risk.value: None/Very Low - Grand Junction is landlocked in western Colorado with no direct hurricane exposure. Occasionally, remnant moisture from decaying Pacific hurricanes/tropical storms (e.g., Hurricane Priscilla remnants in 2025) can bring heavy rain and flash-flood risk, but there is no tropical storm or hurricane wind/surge threat.

wildfire_risk.value: Moderate - First Street Foundation rates Grand Junction's overall wildfire risk as moderate over the next 30 years, with about 38% of properties in the city (over 12,000) having some wildfire exposure; nearby wildland areas see frequent red flag warnings and fires each summer.

earthquake_risk.value: Low-to-moderate - The Colorado Geological Survey rates the state's overall earthquake hazard as low-to-moderate. Grand Junction experiences relatively few earthquakes (about 18-19 per year within 100 km, mostly minor), with occasional magnitude 3-4+ events; there are known local faults (e.g., Redlands Fault, Glade Park Fault) but no major seismic hazard.

flood_risk.value: Moderate to High - Flooding (including snowmelt and flash flooding) is described by Mesa County as the most common natural hazard in the area, occurring on some scale almost every year; most of Grand Junction lies in FEMA Zone X, meaning it is not in the mapped 100-year floodplain but does carry risk from the 500-year flood event and localized flash flooding.

category_summary.value: Grand Junction sits in a relatively low-earthquake zone (18-19/year, mostly minor) with negligible volcano risk, but faces notable flood hazard (the area's most common natural hazard, occurring almost yearly at some scale) and moderate wildfire risk affecting ~38% of properties, plus occasional heavy rain from decaying Pacific hurricanes. Air quality is good (AQI 34) with manageable wildfire smoke (~15 days/year).

cons: Flooding most common hazard, almost yearly
Moderate wildfire risk (38% of properties exposed)

category_summary.value: This location features USDA Zone 7a winters (lows near 19°F) with virtually no snow, hot but not extreme summers peaking at 93.7°F in July, and an excellent 175-day growing season. Expect abundant sunshine (3200 days annually) with moderate rainfall concentrated in fall (1.22" in September) and a dry June (0.47").

primary_water_source.value: The City of Grand Junction's primary treated water supply is surface water from the Kannah Creek watershed on Grand Mesa (snowmelt/reservoirs feeding the Grand Junction and Kannah Creek water plants). Most of the rest of the Grand Valley/Mesa County is served by the Ute Water Conservancy District, whose primary raw-water sources are lakes, reservoirs and streams on the north side of Grand Mesa, with the Colorado River mainstem serving as a secondary/emergency source.

drought_risk.value: High. Mesa County is currently in severe-to-exceptional drought; the winter of 2025-2026 produced the region's worst snowpack on record (roughly 25% of normal in the Colorado headwaters and about 18% in the Gunnison Basin), and Jan-May 2026 was the driest such period in 132 years of record. Colorado's Governor activated Phase 2 of the state's Drought Response Plan in March 2026, and Mesa County water providers have warned of possible watering restrictions.

well_legality.value: Private wells are legal but tightly regulated. Colorado groundwater is presumed tributary to surface streams and administered under the prior-appropriation ('first in time, first in right') doctrine. Mesa County lies outside any Designated Groundwater Basin, so new wells require a permit from the Colorado Division of Water Resources (State Engineer) under C.R.S. §37-90-137, and many uses require an adjudicated water right/augmentation plan. Small-capacity 'exempt' household wells (generally ≤15 gpm) can be permitted for indoor domestic use, but if a municipal or district water tap is reasonably available, state rules typically require obtaining permission from that provider before drilling a private well, and outdoor irrigation from such wells is often restricted or prohibited.

water_rights_restrictions.value: All water use in Colorado, including in Mesa County, is governed by the constitutional prior-appropriation doctrine ('first in time, first in right'): senior decreed water rights must be satisfied before junior rights when supply is short, so newer or undecreed users can be curtailed during dry years. The Colorado and Gunnison Rivers in the Grand Valley are heavily appropriated and subject to interstate Colorado River Compact obligations, making new water rights hard to secure; most groundwater withdrawals require a water court decree and, if they would injure a senior stream call, an approved augmentation plan.

backup_water_availability.value: Backup supply exists but is constrained. Ute Water Conservancy District, which serves most of the Grand Valley, maintains the Colorado River mainstem as a secondary/emergency raw-water source in addition to its primary Grand Mesa reservoirs. The City of Grand Junction is studying conversion of Gunnison River gravel pits into new storage reservoirs to supplement its Kannah Creek watershed supply, which city officials project may need bolstering by around 2039; this project is still in the feasibility/study phase and not yet built. Given the record 2025-2026 drought and low mountain reservoir storage, backup capacity is currently limited, and the City has discussed drought-rate surcharges and watering restrictions if supplies tighten further.

category_summary.value: Grand Junction's water supply faces severe drought stress: record-breaking low snowpack (18–25% of normal) and the driest five-month period in 132 years triggered Phase 2 state drought response in 2026, with municipal suppliers warning of imminent watering restrictions and rate hikes. Backup storage is limited and mostly in planning stages, while new water rights are extremely difficult to obtain under Colorado's prior-appropriation system, making long-term supply security uncertain for relocators.

pros: Well water legally permitted (≤15 gpm exempt)
Backup Colorado River available as emergency source
cons: New water rights extremely hard to secure
Backup storage not yet built (feasibility phase)

grid_reliability.value: Good - served by Grand Valley Power (cooperative) and Public Service Co of Colorado (Xcel Energy), with a 230,000-volt transmission line serving the area and two Tier 1 power providers offering redundant connectivity; recent substation upgrades (e.g., $2.2M Highline Substation upgrade completed 2025) support reliable service, though rural mountain terrain can pose occasional outage risks

net_metering_policy.value: Grand Valley Power (GVP), the local electric cooperative serving Grand Junction/Mesa County, offers net metering under its GEN-1 tariff. Under current rules, monthly excess generation is banked, with an annual true-up. GVP has proposed revisions effective April 1, 2026, changing the annual settlement process so that beginning April 2027, all annual banked excess kWh will be purchased down to zero (paid as a bill credit) rather than carried forward indefinitely, aligning payments with the year generation occurred ahead of a 2028 transition to Guzman Energy as wholesale supplier.

category_summary.value: Electricity costs 12.5 cents/kWh with good grid reliability backed by two Tier 1 providers and recent substation upgrades, though rural mountain terrain can trigger occasional outages. Net metering is available but faces a significant policy shift in 2027 when annual solar credits will no longer roll forward indefinitely—excess generation will be paid out annually instead.

cons: Solar credits won't roll forward after 2027

demographic_breakdown.value: Grand Junction's population is approximately 76% White (non-Hispanic majority), 17% Hispanic, and 4% Two or More Races, with smaller shares of Asian, Black, and Native American residents; county-wide, Mesa County is about 78.4% White, 15.5% Hispanic, and 3.5% Two or More Races.

political_leaning.value: Strongly Republican-leaning/conservative. Mesa County is the largest county on Colorado's Western Slope but votes reliably GOP — Trump won about 61% of the Mesa County vote in the 2024 presidential election, and the area is part of Colorado's 3rd Congressional District (Cook PVI R+5), represented by a Republican.

carry_law_type.value: Shall-issue for concealed carry: Colorado requires a Concealed Handgun Permit (CHP) issued by the county sheriff to carry a concealed firearm in public; the state does not allow permitless/constitutional concealed carry (one narrow exception exists for handguns carried inside a private vehicle). Open carry is generally legal statewide for those 18+ who can legally possess a firearm, though local municipalities may impose additional restrictions in 'sensitive' locations.

firearm_magazine_restrictions.value: Colorado bans magazines capable of holding more than 15 rounds of ammunition ('large-capacity magazines') under C.R.S. §18-12-302, in effect since July 1, 2013; magazines owned before that date are grandfathered but subject to strict handling limits. Violation is generally a class 2 misdemeanor, escalating to a felony if possessed during a violent crime.

self_defense_legal_status.value: Colorado is not a formal 'stand your ground' state by statute, but case law establishes no duty to retreat when using justified force in self-defense, both inside and outside the home. Colorado's 'Make My Day' law (Castle Doctrine, C.R.S. §18-1-704.5), enacted in 1985, allows occupants to use deadly force against an intruder who has unlawfully entered a dwelling if they reasonably believe the intruder intends to commit a crime and use physical force, without any duty to retreat.

category_summary.value: Grand Junction is a mid-sized city of ~71,000 in a heavily Republican county (Trump won 61% in 2024) with above-average violent crime (546.1 per 100k) and notably high property crime (3,170.4 per 100k), though Colorado's 15-round magazine limit and shall-issue concealed carry permitting apply.

cons: Violent crime elevated vs. US average

notable_schools_in_city.value: Grand Junction High School, Central High School, Fruita Monument High School, Palisade High School, and Colorado Mesa University (higher education) are among the most notable institutions serving Grand Junction; Central High School and Fruita Monument High School are currently slated for major facility upgrades under District 51's 25-year, $582M facility master plan.

homeschool_regulation_strictness.value: Low - Colorado is classified as a low-regulation state for homeschooling; parents must simply notify the local school district of intent to homeschool and provide 172 days of instruction per year, with no mandatory curriculum approval, teacher qualification requirements, or standardized testing for most families.

homeschool_co_op_presence.value: Moderate to High - Grand Junction and the broader Grand Valley area host several active homeschool co-ops and support groups (e.g., Grand Valley Homeschool Co-op and church-affiliated groups) that offer group classes, field trips, and social activities, reflecting a well-established homeschooling community typical of Western Slope Colorado.

dominant_hospital_network.value: Intermountain Health (formerly SCL Health) - St. Mary's Medical Center is the dominant hospital network serving Grand Junction and Mesa County, operating as a Level II trauma center and regional referral hub for Western Colorado; Community Hospital is the other significant provider in the market.

category_summary.value: Grand Junction has 44 public schools with a modest 3.5 district rating, though Central and Fruita Monument High Schools are undergoing major upgrades via a $582M facility plan; healthcare is strong with St. Mary's Medical Center—a Level II trauma center—just 0.5 miles away. Homeschooling is minimally regulated (only 172 instruction days required, no curriculum approval needed) with a well-established co-op community.

permitting_strictness.value: Moderate - City has historically had layered zoning/review requirements, but Grand Junction's 2025 Housing Affordability Code Task Force and Proposition 123 commitments are actively streamlining review processes and expediting qualifying housing project approvals.

hoa_prevalence.value: Moderate - Common in newer subdivisions and planned developments around Grand Junction (e.g., Redlands, Orchard Mesa new builds), but many older in-city neighborhoods and rural Mesa County parcels have no HOA.

broadband_availability.value: Good - Grand Junction has multiple wired providers (Charter Spectrum, CenturyLink/Brightspeed, Mesa County's municipal fiber initiatives) offering broadband speeds above 100 Mbps to most of the urban core, though rural Mesa County areas have more limited access.

transit_availability.value: Limited - Grand Valley Transit (GVT) operates a network of fixed-route buses within Grand Junction and connections to Fruita and Palisade, but service frequency is low and the city remains largely car-dependent.

category_summary.value: Grand Junction offers a median home price of $430,000 with moderate permitting that's actively being streamlined, plus good broadband to urban areas (100+ Mbps) and low state income tax at 4.4%. However, the city is car-dependent with a walkability score of just 34 and limited transit options.

raw_dairy_legality.value: Colorado prohibits retail/store sale of raw milk for human consumption, but allows legal access via registered herdshare (cow-share/goat-share) agreements where a consumer co-owns an animal and picks up milk directly from the registered farm/dairy; resale of herdshare milk is strictly prohibited and it cannot be sold at farmers markets, schools, or food establishments.

farmers_market_density.value: Grand Junction/Mesa County has multiple regular farmers markets, including the Downtown 'Market on Main' (weekly Thursday evenings, July–September, on Main Street), the Orchard Mesa Farmers Market (Tuesdays at the Mesa County Fairgrounds), plus additional seasonal markets in nearby Palisade and Fruita, giving the Grand Valley several producer-direct markets within a short drive of the city.

airports_within_2hr.value: Grand Junction Regional Airport (GJT) is located in the city itself (about 5 miles/13 minutes from downtown) and offers commercial flights on United and American to hubs like Denver, Salt Lake City, Dallas/Ft. Worth, Phoenix, Las Vegas, Chicago, and Los Angeles. Montrose Regional Airport (MTJ), roughly 52 miles/about an hour south, is the only other commercial airport within a 2-hour drive; smaller/regional options like Aspen (ASE) and Eagle County (EGE) are just outside 2 hours (~2.5 hours away).

metro_hubs_within_6hr.value: Denver, CO is about 243 miles / roughly 4 hours by car via I-70, and Salt Lake City, UT is about 284-289 miles / roughly 4.5-5.5 hours by car via I-70 and US-6/191, making both major metro hubs reachable within a 6-hour drive from Grand Junction.

category_summary.value: Grand Junction offers convenient local air access via its own regional airport with major hub connections (Denver, Phoenix, Las Vegas, Salt Lake City, Dallas), and easy reach to Denver/Salt Lake City metros within 4–5.5 hours. Grocery shopping is limited to Target and Sprouts locally, with Whole Foods 110 miles away and Costco 118 miles away; however, multiple weekly farmers markets provide producer-direct food access, and raw milk access is available through legal herdshare agreements.

pros: Airport in city with major hub flights
Raw milk legally accessible via herdshare
cons: No Whole Foods, Costco, or Trader Joes within 110+ miles
Limited conventional grocery chains (Target, Sprouts only)

overall_summary.value: Grand Junction, Colorado presents a mixed relocation profile with notable strengths in climate, energy infrastructure, and regional connectivity, but significant challenges in water security and public safety that warrant careful consideration. The city offers excellent growing conditions (175-day season, 3200 annual sunshine days), moderate housing costs ($430,000 median), low state income tax (4.4%), and good access to major regional hubs via its own airport—making it attractive for remote workers and those seeking outdoor lifestyle amenities. However, prospective relocators must confront severe water supply stress stemming from record-low snowpack and an unprecedented five-month drought that triggered Phase 2 state restrictions in 2026, with municipal suppliers warning of imminent rate hikes and watering restrictions that threaten long-term sustainability. Additionally, the city's above-average violent crime rate (546.1 per 100k) and notably high property crime rate (3,170.4 per 100k)—significantly exceeding national averages—present public safety concerns, while car-dependent infrastructure (walkability score 34), limited local grocery options, and modest school district ratings (3.5) further complicate the calculus. The area's natural hazards are relatively manageable (low seismic activity, good air quality), and strong healthcare services via a nearby Level II trauma center are definite assets, but the combination of water scarcity, crime, and limited walkability suggests Grand Junction works best for self-sufficient, security-conscious relocators prioritizing climate and energy considerations over urban amenities and public safety metrics.
